denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Continuous glucose monitor (Dexcom G7) related to Type 2 diabetes mellitus with labile glucose (E11.65).

After clinical review using Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletins (CPBs) and InterQual criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: CGM not evidence-based for Type 2 without intensive insulin per policy. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer cites lack of RCT evidence or non-coverage for indication.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Continuous glucose monitor (Dexcom G7). If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management
clinical_context:
The 59-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes mellitus with labile glucose (E11.65). She has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Continuous glucose monitor (Dexcom G7) as the next appropriate step.

Patient on basal-bolus with nocturnal hypoglycemia despite fingersticks. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on not evidence based without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
